hero_image.caption: Anna Julia Cooper.
hero_image.repository: Library of Congress, C. M. Bell Studio Collection
hero_image.license: Public domain (Library of Congress, no known copyright restrictions; published before 1931)

inline_images.alt: Studio portrait of Anna Julia Cooper as a young woman, late 1880s
inline_images.caption: Anna Julia Cooper, photographed in Oberlin, Ohio around 1888, the educator and scholar whose 1892 A Voice from the South argued for Black women's higher education and leadership.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (LC-DIG-ppmsca-50303)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published before 1929; no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.why: Cooper argues that the education and organized agency of Black women drive racial progress, and Terry credits the women of Bennett College as the deliberate planners of the Greensboro sit-in.

related_chapters.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
related_chapters.part_title: The Only Woman at the Table
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Only Woman at the Table
related_chapters.why: Cooper insists a woman's voice belongs in the councils that shape her people's future, and Cotton recounts holding her place as the only woman in SCLC's inner leadership circle.

related_chapters.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
related_chapters.part_title: Strong Women, and the Deacon Ranks
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Movement Where Women Had a Voice
related_chapters.why: Cooper's central claim is that the race rises only as its women are heard, and this chapter turns directly on the place women held in the Bogalusa movement.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.part_title: Shirley Sherrod and a Family's History
related_chapters.chapter_title: If You Don't Know Your History
related_chapters.topic: Teaching the Young and a Legacy
related_chapters.why: Cooper ties women's leadership to teaching the next generation, and Miller, mother of Shirley Sherrod, speaks of passing history and purpose to the young.

related_chapters.subject: Judy Richardson
related_chapters.why: Cooper frames women as agents rather than bystanders of reform, and this chapter centers on a sit-in carried by women in the SNCC office.

related_chapters.subject: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson)
related_chapters.part_title: Waiting on You All My Life
related_chapters.topic: Two Women Who Took Them In
related_chapters.why: Cooper credits unheralded women as the engine of community uplift, and Simmons, a Freedom Summer project director, names the women who sustained the Laurel project.

top_voice.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
top_voice.reason: Terry joins Cooper's argument that educated Black women are the deliberate authors of progress: a Bennett College student in the Greensboro sit-in who became a chair of Afro-American Studies.

closing_image.alt: Bennett College, Greensboro, North Carolina, 1910 archival photograph
closing_image.caption: Bennett College, Greensboro, North Carolina, 1910 archival photograph. Esther M. A. Terry is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.photographer: Unknown (W. N. Hartshorn and George W. Penniman, eds., institutional source book)

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions A Voice from the South raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson, Marilyn Luper Hildreth, and Ruby Sales.
